content.text: When a Gen AI application produces an output, that output is shaped by three distinct inputs: the user's prompt, the system prompt authored by the application team, and the context chunks injected from retrieval (RAG). Data lineage tracking records the full provenance of every inference: which version of which document contributed which chunk, which model processed them, and which output was produced. Without lineage, you cannot answer a regulator asking 'what data did this response draw on?', a security team asking 'did any RESTRICTED document contribute to a response sent to an unauthorised user?', or a content owner asking 'which responses were influenced by the document I just retracted?'.

This topic covers the data model for prompt and context lineage, the capture points in the inference pipeline, the lineage store schema, and the query patterns used for auditing, incident response, and data subject access requests.

attrs.alt: Prompt and context lineage tracking pipeline

content.text: Why Prompt Lineage Is Harder Than Traditional Data Lineage

Traditional ETL lineage tracks how a column in table A became a column in table B through a deterministic transformation. Gen AI lineage is non-deterministic in two ways: the same prompt with the same retrieved context will produce different outputs on each call (temperature > 0), and the model's internal computation is not interpretable. This means lineage for Gen AI cannot capture 'how the output was derived' at the token level — it can only capture 'what inputs were present when the output was generated'.
Prompt lineage records what was sent to the model, not how the model used it
Context lineage records which source documents contributed chunks, not which chunks influenced which tokens
Model lineage records which model version processed the request, since fine-tuned models may have memorised training data
Output lineage links the response text back to the full input set so that retraction of a source document can trigger review of all affected responses

Design principle: lineage tracking records factual causation (these inputs were present) rather than claimed causation (these inputs produced this output). This is both technically accurate and legally defensible.

from __future__ import annotations
import hashlib, json, time, uuid
from dataclasses import dataclass, field, asdict
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional

class PromptRole(str, Enum):
    SYSTEM    = "system"
    USER      = "user"
    ASSISTANT = "assistant"
    TOOL      = "tool"

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class PromptSegment:
    """One message in the conversation turn sent to the model."""
    role:           PromptRole
    content_hash:   str        # SHA-256 of the raw content string
    char_count:     int
    sensitivity_label: str     # from classification pipeline
    is_rag_injected: bool      # True if content was injected by the RAG pipeline

    @classmethod
    def from_content(cls, role: PromptRole, content: str,
                     label: str, is_rag: bool) -> "PromptSegment":
        return cls(
            role=role,
            content_hash=hashlib.sha256(content.encode()).hexdigest(),
            char_count=len(content),
            sensitivity_label=label,
            is_rag_injected=is_rag,
        )

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class ContextSourceRef:
    """Reference to one document chunk injected into the prompt via RAG."""
    source_doc_id:    str       # document ID in the knowledge base
    source_doc_version: str     # version/hash of the document at retrieval time
    chunk_index:      int       # position of the chunk within the document
    retrieval_score:  float     # cosine similarity score at retrieval time
    collection_name:  str       # vector store collection
    sensitivity_label: str

@dataclass
class PromptLineageRecord:
    """Root lineage record; one per inference call."""
    lineage_id:       str = field(default_factory=lambda: str(uuid.uuid4()))
    request_id:       str = ""
    session_id:       str = ""
    tenant_id:        str = ""
    user_id:          str = ""
    timestamp_utc:    float = field(default_factory=time.time)
    model_id:         str = ""          # e.g. "claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022"
    model_endpoint:   str = ""          # full HTTPS URL
    prompt_segments:  list[PromptSegment] = field(default_factory=list)
    context_sources:  list[ContextSourceRef] = field(default_factory=list)
    max_sensitivity:  str = "PUBLIC"    # ceiling label across all segments + sources
    token_count_in:   int = 0
    token_count_out:  int = 0
    response_hash:    str = ""          # SHA-256 of response text

    def add_segment(self, seg: PromptSegment) -> None:
        self.prompt_segments.append(seg)

    def add_context_source(self, src: ContextSourceRef) -> None:
        self.context_sources.append(src)

    def to_ndjson_line(self) -> str:
        return json.dumps(asdict(self), default=str)
Capture Points in the Inference Pipeline
Lineage records must be created at the application layer, not inside the model API. The capture points are the same places where the application constructs the prompt and receives the response:
session_id, tenant_id, user_id, user clearance level
When the system prompt template is rendered
ContextSourceRef for every chunk returned by the vector store, including score and document version
After retrieval, before injection into prompt
When the user message arrives at the API layer
Finalise PromptLineageRecord with model_id, endpoint, token_count_in
Immediately before sending to the model API
Set response_hash, token_count_out; write record to lineage store
Immediately after receiving the model response

from __future__ import annotations
import hashlib, time
from functools import wraps
from typing import Callable, Any
from lineage import (PromptLineageRecord, PromptSegment, ContextSourceRef,
                     PromptRole, LineageStore)
from classification import classify_text   # from classification pipeline

class LineageMiddleware:
    """Wraps an LLM client to capture lineage on every call."""

    def __init__(self, llm_client: Any, lineage_store: "LineageStore"):
        self.client = llm_client
        self.store  = lineage_store

    def chat(
        self,
        messages: list[dict],
        model: str,
        endpoint: str,
        context_sources: list[ContextSourceRef],
        session_id: str,
        tenant_id: str,
        user_id: str,
        request_id: str,
    ) -> tuple[str, str]:
        """Returns (response_text, lineage_id)."""
        record = PromptLineageRecord(
            request_id=request_id,
            session_id=session_id,
            tenant_id=tenant_id,
            user_id=user_id,
            model_id=model,
            model_endpoint=endpoint,
        )

        # Capture each message segment
        for msg in messages:
            label = classify_text(msg["content"]).label.value
            is_rag = msg.get("_rag_injected", False)
            seg = PromptSegment.from_content(
                role=PromptRole(msg["role"]),
                content=msg["content"],
                label=label,
                is_rag=is_rag,
            )
            record.add_segment(seg)

        # Attach context source refs
        for src in context_sources:
            record.add_context_source(src)

        # Compute maximum sensitivity label
        all_labels = [s.sensitivity_label for s in record.prompt_segments] +                      [s.sensitivity_label for s in record.context_sources]
        record.max_sensitivity = max(all_labels, default="PUBLIC")

        # Make the actual LLM call
        response_text = self.client.chat(messages=messages, model=model)
        record.response_hash = hashlib.sha256(response_text.encode()).hexdigest()
        record.token_count_out = len(response_text.split())  # approximate; use API token count if available

        # Persist lineage
        self.store.append(record)
        return response_text, record.lineage_id

The lineage store is append-only. Records are written once and never mutated — the same constraint as the routing audit log. For most enterprise deployments, lineage records are stored in an NDJSON file per day (rotated at midnight UTC) and indexed by a secondary key-value store mapping lineage_id → file + byte_offset for O(1) lookup.

When a source document is retracted — because it contained an error, was classified upward, or was subject to a data subject erasure request — the lineage store allows you to identify every response that was generated while that document was in the knowledge base. This is the core use case that justifies the engineering cost of lineage tracking.

from __future__ import annotations
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional
from lineage_store import LineageStore

@dataclass
class RetractionImpactReport:
    source_doc_id:      str
    affected_lineage_ids: list[str]
    affected_session_ids: set[str]
    affected_user_ids:   set[str]
    max_sensitivity:     str
    recommended_action:  str

def analyse_retraction_impact(
    store: LineageStore,
    source_doc_id: str,
) -> RetractionImpactReport:
    affected_ids: list[str] = []
    sessions: set[str] = set()
    users: set[str] = set()
    labels: list[str] = []

    for record in store.find_by_source_doc(source_doc_id):
        affected_ids.append(record.lineage_id)
        sessions.add(record.session_id)
        users.add(record.user_id)
        labels.append(record.max_sensitivity)

    max_label = max(labels, default="PUBLIC")

    if max_label in ("REGULATED", "CONFIDENTIAL"):
        action = (
            f"Document contributed to {len(affected_ids)} responses across "
            f"{len(users)} users. HIGH RISK: notify DPO, review each response "
            f"for data subject impact, consider notifying affected users."
        )
    elif max_label == "RESTRICTED":
        action = (
            f"Document contributed to {len(affected_ids)} responses. "
            f"Review for unauthorised disclosure. Update knowledge base and "
            f"re-index if corrected version is available."
        )
    else:
        action = (
            f"Document contributed to {len(affected_ids)} responses. "
            f"Update knowledge base. No regulatory notification required."
        )

    return RetractionImpactReport(
        source_doc_id=source_doc_id,
        affected_lineage_ids=affected_ids,
        affected_session_ids=sessions,
        affected_user_ids=users,
        max_sensitivity=max_label,
        recommended_action=action,
    )
Data Subject Access Requests and Lineage

Under GDPR Article 15, a data subject can request information about all automated processing of their personal data, including processing by AI systems. For a Gen AI application, the lineage store enables a complete response: every session in which the data subject's personal data was present in the prompt, which model processed it, which external documents were retrieved as context, and what response was generated.
Join lineage record with application purpose register
Extract hostname from endpoint URL across all records
Aggregate unique source_doc_ids across all user sessions
lineage store retention is max(GDPR 7 yr, HIPAA 6 yr)
If response triggered an automated action, link via request_id to action log

Lineage records themselves contain personal data (user_id, session_id, and potentially prompt content hashes that could be linked back to individuals). They must be retained long enough to support regulatory enquiries and DSAR responses, but purged when the applicable retention period expires.

from __future__ import annotations
from datetime import datetime, timezone, timedelta
from pathlib import Path

RETENTION_DAYS: dict[str, int] = {
    "REGULATED":    365 * 7,   # GDPR: 7 years (financial sector default)
    "CONFIDENTIAL": 365 * 5,
    "RESTRICTED":   365 * 3,
    "INTERNAL":     365 * 1,
    "PUBLIC":       180,
}

def purge_expired_records(base_dir: Path, dry_run: bool = True) -> dict[str, int]:
    """
    Scan lineage files and remove records past their retention period.
    Returns counts of retained vs purged records per file.
    dry_run=True logs what would be purged without modifying files.
    """
    import json
    now = datetime.now(tz=timezone.utc)
    summary: dict[str, int] = {"retained": 0, "purged": 0}

    for ndjson_file in sorted(base_dir.glob("lineage_*.ndjson")):
        retained_lines: list[str] = []
        for line in ndjson_file.read_text().splitlines():
            if not line.strip():
                continue
            data = json.loads(line)
            label = data.get("max_sensitivity", "PUBLIC")
            ts = data.get("timestamp_utc", 0)
            record_dt = datetime.fromtimestamp(ts, tz=timezone.utc)
            cutoff = now - timedelta(days=RETENTION_DAYS.get(label, 180))
            if record_dt > cutoff:
                retained_lines.append(line)
                summary["retained"] += 1
            else:
                summary["purged"] += 1
                if not dry_run:
                    pass  # line is simply not appended to retained_lines

        if not dry_run:
            ndjson_file.write_text("\n".join(retained_lines) + ("\n" if retained_lines else ""))
    return summary

Lineage and observability (tracing / metrics) serve related but distinct purposes in a Gen AI stack. Confusing them leads to either over-collecting (storing full prompt text in an observability backend with weak access controls) or under-collecting (relying only on observability traces to answer regulatory questions they were not designed for).
Compliance, DPO, legal, data subject requests
Content hashes, source doc refs, model IDs — no raw text
Spans, latencies, token counts, error rates — may include truncated text in debug mode
Point lookup by lineage_id; batch scan by source_doc_id
Yes — submitted in DSAR responses and audit packages

Prompt and context lineage tracking is the evidence layer that links every Gen AI response back to the exact inputs that produced it: the user prompt segments, the RAG-retrieved document chunks with their source references and retrieval scores, the model version, and the endpoint. The lineage store is append-only and day-partitioned, enabling efficient lookup by lineage_id, session, user, or source document. The document retraction use case — finding all responses that drew on a document that must now be removed — is the clearest demonstration of lineage value. The DSAR response use case converts lineage records into the legally-required disclosure of automated processing under GDPR Article 15. Retention policy aligns lineage lifetimes with the sensitivity label of the highest-classified input, with a maximum of seven years for regulated data.
